role_summary: President of the San Francisco NAACP; head pastor of Third Baptist Church of San Francisco, 1976-2025.
ai_reading: Amos C. Brown's testimony sits closer in cosine similarity to Charles McLaurin's (entry 17) Mississippi voter-registration testimony (count 161) than to that of any other figure in the corpus, ahead of Clarence B. Jones (entry 20), Samuel Berry McKinney (entry 115), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108). The cultural reading of Brown would have predicted his San Francisco NAACP pastorate and SCLC-style ministry to land him near other Bay Area or West Coast religious figures; instead the embedding pulls his testimony toward the Mississippi grassroots organizers, suggesting the Jackson birth, Morehouse education, and 1961 Freedom Rides arrest dominate the vector signature over the half-century San Francisco pulpit. On the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis he lands at -0.56, well into the tactical end, the unexpected position for a long-serving senior pastor whose institutional posture would predict strategic-vision testimony; possibly an artifact of his oral history's focus on specific 1955-1961 movement actions over later abstract reflection.

photo.alt: Amos C. Brown, 1998, during his term on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors

biographical_paragraph: Eight students sat in a social-philosophy seminar at Morehouse College that Martin Luther King Jr. co-taught with the campus minister Samuel Woodrow Williams; Amos C. Brown was one of them [src: 1, 7]. The path to that classroom began in Jackson, Mississippi, where Brown was born February 20, 1941, and ran through a 1956 meeting with King at the NAACP's national convention in San Francisco, where the fifteen-year-old had traveled with Medgar Evers [src: 1, 7]. The year before that meeting, Brown organized a Jackson NAACP youth council, and by 1959 he chaired the NAACP's National Youth Work Committee [src: 1, 7]. As a Morehouse student he joined the Atlanta sit-in movement, co-drafting the Appeal for Human Rights, and was jailed in October 1960 alongside King after a downtown department-store sit-in [src: 1, 8]. In the summer of 1961 he took part in the Freedom Rides, traveling on his own to Jackson, where he was arrested during protests there [src: 1, 4]. After a 1964 Morehouse bachelor's degree, taken with a recommendation letter King wrote on his behalf, he earned an M.Div. at Crozer Theological Seminary, King's own alma mater, and later a D.Min. at United Theological Seminary [src: 1]; Third Baptist Church of San Francisco was his pulpit from 1976 onward, the pastorate running parallel to his presidency of the city's NAACP branch [src: 1, 2]. The Civil Rights History Project archives his oral history at the Library of Congress as entry 2 [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Brown traces his entry into the movement to a Jackson youth council he started at fourteen, and to Medgar Evers arranging his first trip out of Mississippi:
interview_snippets.quote: That I got involved and I organized the first Youth Council of NAACP in 1955. And after I organized that Youth Council, that spring of 1956, Medgar Evers asked my mother if I could travel with him to San Francisco,

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling his 1959 term chairing the NAACP's National Youth Work Committee, Brown dates the student sit-in movement earlier than its usual Greensboro origin:
interview_snippets.quote: You see, in this country, we have romanticized the student city movement. The student city movement did not begin in Greensboro, 1960. It started in Oklahoma City, August 28th, 1958.

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the Atlanta student sit-in movement he joined as a Morehouse student, after the group drafted the Appeal for Human Rights:
interview_snippets.quote: We sat in, and I spent time in jail with Dr. Martin Luther King, his brother, A.D. [King], Otis Moss, still alive,

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked about the small Morehouse social-philosophy seminar King co-taught, Brown describes the room:
interview_snippets.quote: Oh, that was an exciting class. It was small, only eight of us. And Dr. King was very down to earth.

movement_context: Third Baptist Church, the institution Brown pastored for nearly half a century, is the oldest Black Baptist congregation west of the Mississippi River, founded in San Francisco in 1852 by free Black Californians during the Gold Rush settlement period [src: 2]. The pastorate Brown inherited in 1976 carried that nineteenth-century institutional weight: Third Baptist had hosted Frederick Douglass for an address during his 1888 West Coast tour and would host both Thurgood Marshall and Martin Luther King Jr. during the twentieth-century civil rights years [src: 2]. The NAACP youth council Brown organized in Jackson in 1955 (at age fourteen, the year before his San Francisco meeting with King) was part of the youth-council organizing that the national NAACP carried across the Deep South in the late-1950s lead-up to the lunch-counter sit-in wave; by 1959 Brown chaired the NAACP's National Youth Work Committee [src: 1, 3]. The jailing alongside King that Brown describes came in October 1960 through the Atlanta student sit-in movement, where, as a Morehouse undergraduate, he helped draft the Appeal for Human Rights and was arrested in a downtown department-store sit-in, distinct from his later Mississippi arrests [src: 1, 8]. In the summer of 1961 Brown took part in the Freedom Rides, an extension of the desegregation campaign that continued after the May 14, 1961 Klan firebombing of a Greyhound bus outside Anniston, Alabama, an event that drew the Kennedy Justice Department into providing federal protection to the riders; rather than join the ride to Montgomery, Brown traveled on his own to Jackson and was arrested during local protests there [src: 1, 4].
legacy_and_reception: Brown served on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors from 1996 to 2001, appointed initially by Mayor Willie Brown and subsequently elected to a full term [src: 5]. In 2020 California Governor Gavin Newsom appointed Brown to the California Task Force to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans, the first state-level reparations study commission in the United States; Brown served as vice-chair through the task force's June 2023 final report [src: 6]. He retired from the Third Baptist pulpit in 2025 after a forty-nine-year pastorate, the longest pastorate in the church's hundred-seventy-three-year history [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history at catalog entry 2 was recorded March 2, 2013 by interviewer David P. Cline; Cline conducted thirty-one of the CRHP's one hundred thirty-five oral histories, the largest interviewer-attributable corpus subset within the archive [src: 1].

gallery.alt: Amos C. Brown at a press conference outside Grace Cathedral, San Francisco, June 26, 2013
gallery.caption: Brown, San Francisco NAACP president, speaking at a press conference of Bay Area religious leaders outside Grace Cathedral, June 26, 2013, the day the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in United States v. Windsor (striking down section 3 of the Defense of Marriage Act) and Hollingsworth v. Perry (effectively reinstating same-sex marriage in California).

gallery.alt: Amos C. Brown speaking at the San Francisco MLK Day interfaith ceremony, January 16, 2017
gallery.caption: Brown speaking at the San Francisco Martin Luther King Jr. Day interfaith ceremony, January 16, 2017. The annual interfaith service is a long-standing pastoral commitment of his that ran in continuous succession from the early years of his Third Baptist pastorate through to his 2025 retirement.
